sections.about: This metric measures the percentage of employees trained on skill upgradation under BRSR Principle 3, Essential Indicator 8. Skill-upgradation training can include internal training courses as well as funding support for external training or education. A higher value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Tata and JSW maintained 100% employee skill-upgradation training coverage across all three years. Jindal also reported 100% in FY2022-23 and FY2024-25, but dipped sharply to 32.21% in FY2023-24; SAIL improved from 32.6% in FY2022-23 to 50.04% in FY2023-24, then fell to 34% in FY2024-25. Tata’s result is backed by a multi-dimensional capability ecosystem covering core foundational knowledge, functional/technical skills, business and leadership skills, digital technology, sustainability and agility; it also uses frameworks such as functional competency development, Schools of Excellence, StepUP and worker-focused E4 training models. Jindal’s FY2024-25 rebound is consistent with its disclosed L&D architecture: 1,621 programmes for employees other than BoD/KMPs, workplace skills, POSH, ethics, time management, leadership development and ESG/industry knowledge. SAIL recognises training as an opportunity for operational efficiency and productivity but the reported employee skill coverage still trails private peers.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata Steel is Rank 1 at 100%, tied with JSW and Jindal. The gap from best performer is 0 percentage points; SAIL trails Tata by 66 percentage points. Tata has held Rank 1 across all three years, which is strategically important because employee skill development supports transition needs around BF-BOF process optimisation, digitalisation, safety risk reduction, maintenance reliability, and future low-carbon steelmaking capabilities.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Tata Steel data is reported on a consolidated basis across all three financial years. All peers report on a standalone basis.
